basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-ʿAṣr — “The Declining Day” — is famous for its brevity. Three verses. And yet, al-Shāfiʿī said, “were the people to ponder this sūra, it would suffice them.” The sūra states the human condition in four words: man is in loss. And it states the path out in four more: save those who believe, do righteous deeds, exhort one another to truth, and exhort one another to patience. Qushayrī, on loss (khusrān), specifies: “The loss that reaches man is of two orders. In deeds — which becomes apparent at the end. In states — which already shows itself in the moment: the contraction (qabḍ, قَبْض) after expansion (basṭ, بَسْط), being veiled after being near, returning to dispensations after preferring the harder and better path.” There is thus a loss one carries without noticing: the silent draining of élan. And there is a way out — through shared faithfulness, and through patience.

versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “al-ʿaṣr is the dahr, time itself — He swears by it. It is also said that He means by it the afternoon prayer (ṣalāt al-ʿaṣr); also: the evening.” The word comes from ʿaṣara, to press — as one presses a fruit for its juice. Time presses in two senses: it flows quickly, and it presses upon the servant, requiring that he make something of it. God swears by this pressure of time as a weighty witness. What follows will say what is meant when one has grasped its urgency.

mots.def: ʿaṣr: the declining afternoon, and more broadly time itself, the era. From the root ʿ-ṣ-r: to press, to squeeze.

versets.commentaire: khusr (خُسْر), loss. Qushayrī divides it in two: the loss of deeds, only seen at the end; and the loss of states, which is seen already today — when the soul passes from expansion (basṭ) to contraction (qabḍ), from nearness to distance, from striving to slackening. Before life ends, the soul knows this loss: what beat strongly one morning falls silent the next; what was clear one evening grows tangled; the practices that had once been life become a weight. The sūra says, with exceptional emphasis (two particles: inna and la-), that there is no escape from this verdict — save by the way named in the next verse.

mots.def: insān: the human being, the generic man.
khusr: loss, deficit — an image of trade in which one spends more than one gains.

versets.ar: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ
versets.tr: illā lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti wa-tawāṣaw bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-tawāṣaw bi-ṣ-ṣabr
versets.trad: save those who believe, perform righteous deeds, exhort one another to truth, and exhort one another to patience.
versets.commentaire: Four conditions, to be heard one by one. Āmanū — they believed; ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāt — they did the righteous deeds. But Qushayrī opens the next two to the Sufi way. Tawāṣaw bi-l-ḥaqq, “they exhorted one another to the Truth”: “That is preferential gift toward creatures (īthār, إِيثَار), and truthfulness with the Truth (ṣidq, صِدْق).” To give to others what one would have kept for oneself, and to be true with what is. And the last, tawāṣaw bi-ṣ-ṣabr, “they exhorted one another to patience”: “Patience in well-being, says Qushayrī — there is no patience more complete.” Surprising: one expected patience in trial. But patience in health, in prosperity, in calm — that is the hard thing. And he adds: “Patience with God. And that is the highest degree of patience.” Tustarī said it otherwise: “Patience is to action what the head is to the body: each is useless without the other.” The sūra, in three lines, thus gives the whole path: believe, act, share the truth, hold gently — especially when nothing forces you to hold.

mots.def: āmana: to believe; bound to amn (security) — the faith that brings rest to the heart.
ṣāliḥāt: good deeds; substantivised feminine plural active participle.

mots.sens: and they have commended to one another
mots.def: tawāṣā: reciprocal form of waṣṣā (to commend, to transmit as testament).
mots.gram: reciprocal perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ḥaqq: truth, the real, what is due; one of the Divine Names.

mots.gram: reciprocal perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: ṣabr: patience, endurance, constancy; according to Qushayrī, above all patience in well-being and patience with God.
